note.soap:
S: Patient reports a tick bite with an associated rash present for 24-72 hours. No fever reported. Location is Georgia. Pregnancy is not possible.

O: Unable to perform physical exam due to phone-only encounter. No vital signs available.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease given tick bite, rash duration, and endemic area (Georgia).  
  - Differential:  
    1. Early Lyme disease (erythema migrans)  
    2. Localized tick bite reaction (non-infectious)  
    3. Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI)  
    4. Cellulitis or other skin infection  
  - Red flags considered: No fever, no systemic symptoms, no signs of severe allergic reaction or neurological symptoms.

P:  
  - Disposition: Routine follow-up with primary care or urgent in-person evaluation if symptoms worsen or systemic signs develop.  
  - Plan: Educate patient on signs of progression including fever, spreading rash, joint pain, or neurological symptoms. Consider empiric antibiotic treatment per local guidelines if erythema migrans is classic and no contraindications.  
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care if fever develops, rash rapidly expands, neurological symptoms (headache, facial palsy), or severe allergic reactions occur.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
